title: Dog Muscle Wasting in Hindquarters: Causes, Urgency, and Care
meta_desc: Noticeable muscle loss in your dog's back legs? Learn about hindquarter atrophy, urgency levels, and how to help your vet diagnose the cause.
content_html:
TL;DR: Hindquarter muscle wasting is a gradual thinning of the rear legs typically caused by underlying pain or nerve issues. While not an emergency, you should schedule a veterinary exam within a few days to diagnose the cause and prevent further mobility loss.

What is hindquarter muscle wasting in dogs and what does it look like?

Muscle wasting, clinically known as atrophy, occurs when the muscle fibers in your dog's rear legs and hip area begin to shrink or thin. You may notice your dog's spine or hip bones appearing more prominent, or their back legs looking significantly thinner than their front legs. This is generally a symptom of an underlying issue—such as chronic pain, nerve dysfunction, or a lack of use—rather than a primary disease itself.

Is hindquarter muscle wasting in my dog a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low.
  • Muscle wasting is typically a gradual process and is rarely an immediate medical emergency.
  • This condition indicates that your dog is likely experiencing discomfort or a loss of mobility that requires professional evaluation.
  • While you do not need to visit an emergency clinic tonight, you should schedule a diagnostic appointment with your primary veterinarian within the next few days to prevent further decline.

How can providing a photo or video help my veterinarian triage my dog?

  • Visual documentation is incredibly valuable for your veterinary team to assess the severity of the condition.
  • A photo taken from directly above your dog can clearly show the symmetry, or lack thereof, in the thigh muscles.
  • A video of your dog walking helps the vet see if they are dragging their toes or favoring one side.
  • These visual aids allow the vet to gauge the severity of the atrophy and prioritize your appointment based on the visible loss of function.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Rapidly developing atrophy of the epaxial and gluteal muscles, indicating hindquarter muscle wasting, can be a sign of immune-mediated polymyositis in dogs, although primarily reported in horses. Muscle biopsies of the epaxial and gluteal muscles may show lymphocytic vasculitis, anguloid atrophy, myofiber infiltration with lymphocytes, fiber necrosis with macrophage infiltration, and regeneration in acute stages. Treatment may involve immunosuppression with dexamethasone (0.05 mg/kg for 3 days), followed by prednisolone (1 mg/kg for 7-10 days), tapered by 100 mg/wk over 1 month if indicated, although the original text discusses the treatment of horses.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1202)

faqs.question: Can old age cause muscle wasting in dogs?
faqs.answer: Yes, sarcopenia is age-related muscle loss, but it is often accelerated by underlying arthritis or decreased activity due to pain.

faqs.question: Is muscle wasting in dogs reversible?
faqs.answer: In many cases, yes. Depending on the cause, physical therapy, improved nutrition, and pain management can help rebuild lost muscle mass.

faqs.question: What are common causes of hind leg atrophy?
faqs.answer: Common causes include osteoarthritis, hip dysplasia, cruciate ligament tears, and neurological conditions like Degenerative Myelopathy.
